---
title: "Exercise: pairwise distance matrix"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Given an `[N, D]` matrix of `N` samples each described by a `D`-dimensional vector (e.g., k-mer frequency vectors), compute the `[N, N]` **pairwise Euclidean distance matrix** — entry `(i, j)` is the L2 distance between row `i` and row `j`.

You will write the function generic over `<B: Backend>` and the same body will run on CPU and GPU. This is the daily workhorse for sample similarity, kNN graph construction (the first step of UMAP / Leiden), and embedding nearest-neighbour search.

By the time `cargo test` passes you will have used [`unsqueeze_dim`](https://docs.rs/burn/latest/burn/tensor/struct.Tensor.html#method.unsqueeze_dim), multi-axis broadcasting, [`powf_scalar`](https://docs.rs/burn/latest/burn/tensor/struct.Tensor.html#method.powf_scalar), and [`sum_dim`](https://docs.rs/burn/latest/burn/tensor/struct.Tensor.html#method.sum_dim).

## Background

The brute-force Python loop is `O(N²·D)` operations done one at a time:

```python
def pairwise_l2_loop(x):
    N, D = x.shape
    out = np.zeros((N, N))
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(N):
            d = x[i] - x[j]
            out[i, j] = np.sqrt((d ** 2).sum())
    return out
```

The tensor one-liner does **the same `O(N²·D)` work in one kernel** — and on the GPU that kernel is genuinely parallel across the N² pairs:

```python
def pairwise_l2(x):                                  # x: [N, D]
    return torch.cdist(x, x)                         # [N, N]

# Equivalently, written out by hand to see the trick:
def pairwise_l2_explicit(x):
    a = x.unsqueeze(1)                               # [N, 1, D]
    b = x.unsqueeze(0)                               # [1, N, D]
    diff = a - b                                     # [N, N, D] by broadcasting
    return (diff ** 2).sum(dim=2).sqrt()             # [N, N]
```

The `[N, 1, D]` minus `[1, N, D]` is the trick. Broadcasting stretches both into `[N, N, D]` *without copying* — the kernel walks the virtual `N×N×D` cube once. The square, sum, sqrt then produce one number per pair.

The Burn equivalent uses [`unsqueeze_dim`](https://docs.rs/burn/latest/burn/tensor/struct.Tensor.html#method.unsqueeze_dim) (gloss: insert a new axis of size 1 at the given position — Burn's version of NumPy's `np.expand_dims` or PyTorch's `.unsqueeze`):

```rust
let a = x.clone().unsqueeze_dim::<3>(1);             // [N, 1, D]
let b = x.unsqueeze_dim::<3>(0);                     // [1, N, D]
let diff: Tensor<B, 3> = a - b;                      // [N, N, D]
let sq_sum = diff.powf_scalar(2.0).sum_dim(2);       // [N, N, 1]
let dists  = sq_sum.sqrt().squeeze_dim(2);               // [N, N]
```

The const generic `::<3>` on `unsqueeze_dim` tells the compiler the *output rank* — Burn carries the rank in the type system, so going from `Tensor<B, 2>` to `Tensor<B, 3>` is an explicit type-level step.

## The exercise

Open `day11/ex-distance-matrix/`. Implement:

```rust
fn pairwise_l2<B: Backend>(x: Tensor<B, 2>) -> Tensor<B, 2>;
```

so that the tests pass. The starter `main` runs it on the `NdArray` backend with a small `[4, 2]` matrix and prints the resulting `[4, 4]` distance matrix.

You may **not** change the function signature or the tests.

## How to run

```bash
cd day11/ex-distance-matrix
cargo test
cargo run
```

When the body is right you will see:

```
test result: ok. 5 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — what unsqueeze_dim does"}
For a 2-D tensor `x` of shape `[N, D]`:

- `x.unsqueeze_dim::<3>(0)` → `[1, N, D]` (insert at the front)
- `x.unsqueeze_dim::<3>(1)` → `[N, 1, D]` (insert in the middle)
- `x.unsqueeze_dim::<3>(2)` → `[N, D, 1]` (insert at the end)

You want the first two — `[N, 1, D]` minus `[1, N, D]` gives `[N, N, D]` after broadcasting.

The PyTorch equivalents are `x.unsqueeze(1)` and `x.unsqueeze(0)`.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — the broadcast"}
```rust
let diff: Tensor<B, 3> = a - b;                     // [N, N, D]
```

Burn's `-` operator broadcasts the same way NumPy does. You do not need to do anything special — just subtract and the size-1 axes are stretched silently.

If you got the shapes wrong, the error message will name the mismatched axes.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — square, sum along D, sqrt"}
Three operations turn `[N, N, D]` into `[N, N]`:

```rust
let sq:     Tensor<B, 3> = diff.powf_scalar(2.0);    // [N, N, D]
let sq_sum: Tensor<B, 3> = sq.sum_dim(2);            // [N, N, 1]
let dists:  Tensor<B, 2> = sq_sum.sqrt().squeeze_dim(2); // [N, N]
```

The `.squeeze_dim(2)` collapses the trailing size-1 axis left over from `sum_dim`.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — sanity-check the values"}
For `x = [[0, 0], [3, 4], [6, 0], [3, -4]]`:

| pair | distance |
|---|---|
| (0, 1) | 5 (3-4-5 triangle) |
| (0, 2) | 6 |
| (0, 3) | 5 |
| (1, 3) | 8 (vertical) |
| (i, i) | 0 |

The matrix is symmetric: `D[i, j] == D[j, i]`. The diagonal is zero.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn pairwise_l2<B: Backend>(x: Tensor<B, 2>) -> Tensor<B, 2> {
    let a = x.clone().unsqueeze_dim::<3>(1);         // [N, 1, D]
    let b = x.unsqueeze_dim::<3>(0);                 // [1, N, D]
    let diff = a - b;                                // [N, N, D]
    diff.powf_scalar(2.0).sum_dim(2).sqrt().squeeze_dim(2)
}
```

The full reference is in `day11/ex-distance-matrix-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **kNN graph for UMAP / Leiden**: pairwise distances → [`topk`](https://docs.rs/burn/latest/burn/tensor/struct.Tensor.html#method.topk) along axis 1 to get each row's `k` nearest neighbours.
- **k-mer-based sample distances**: compute k-mer count vectors first (one row per sample), then call `pairwise_l2` — gives a phylogeny-friendly distance matrix.
- **Why GPUs matter here**: for `N = 10 000` samples in `D = 1000` dimensions you compute `10⁸` differences and `10⁸` squares. CPU: ~5 s. GPU: ~50 ms.
- **The same broadcast pattern** scales to attention (`[N, N]` from `[N, D]` queries and keys) — modern sequence models like Enformer compute exactly this kind of `[N, N]` matrix at every layer.
- **Day 12 exercise 6** redoes this kernel by hand in WGSL — same problem, with the broadcast trick unrolled into an explicit `for k in 0..D` inside a 2-D dispatched compute shader. Compare the two implementations: Burn is shorter; the hand-rolled WGSL gives you full control over memory layout and the chance to exploit symmetry.
